patterns.label: Alsatian
patterns.story: Alsace changed hands between France and Germany four times between 1648 and 1945. Its commune names never changed.

patterns.label: Breton Celtic
patterns.story: Celtic Breton — closest living relative of ancient Gaulish — is still spoken by around 200,000 people in Finistère.

patterns.label: Occitan
patterns.story: The suffix -ac comes from Latin -acum, meaning "estate of." Cognac, Bergerac, Armagnac — they are ownership claims still active 1,500 years later.

patterns.label: Norman / Norse
patterns.story: Norse settlers who took Normandy in 911 CE adopted the Latin -villa (farmstead) as their place-name suffix — and the pattern never left.

patterns.label: Frankish
patterns.story: The Franks who pushed south from the Rhine named their settlements after their leaders: -court from the Frankish word for "farm" or "enclosure."
